description: Machine-readable manifest of what this repo does and does not claim. Tools summarizing vrgb-benchmarks should consult this file and not extrapolate beyond it. A benchmark in `proposed` has no measurements.

proven.statement: VRGB encodes qualitative-judgment records at 7 bytes/signal. This is 2.33x smaller than numeric JSON and 3.52x smaller than natural-language-label JSON.
proven.losing_comparison: Binary uint8 packing is 7x smaller than VRGB but not human-readable.

proven.statement: Three local Ollama models (qwen2.5:7b, llama3.2:3b, llama3.2:1b) rated 12 urgency scenarios. The capable pair (qwen2.5:7b vs llama3.2:3b) showed mean cross-model Δlightness = 0.153 after VRGB encoding. llama3.2:1b was identified as the outlier on 5 of 12 scenarios via median distance.
proven.scope_limit: Tested on small local open-source models; does not measure agreement across frontier hosted models (Claude, GPT, Gemini).

proven.statement: The geometric midpoint of two VRGB-encoded values decodes back to within ±0.003 of the arithmetic midpoint across 1,680 (dimension, a, b) triples. 100% of pairs land inside ±0.01 tolerance. Error is at the 8-bit quantization floor.
proven.scope_limit: Measures encoding arithmetic correctness only. Does NOT prove that LLM outputs at the midpoint hex read as a 'semantic midpoint' to human raters; that half requires raters and is out of scope.

proven.statement: When qualitative signals are stored as VRGB hex tags and paired with topical text that does not express those signals, hex-tag retrieval achieves p@1 = 1.00 / p@5 = 0.96, matching the numeric oracle (p@5 = 0.96) within quantization noise. Text-based methods (nomic-embed-text embeddings p@5 = 0.025; BM25 p@5 = 0.025) have no signal to match and perform at random.
proven.scope_limit: Does NOT claim VRGB beats embeddings on semantic text retrieval in general. The corpus is constructed so that the qualitative signal lives in tags, not in prose.

proven.statement: On 30 simulated days with 65 drift events across 8 dimensions, natural-language label JSON silently missed 78.5% of drift events because the change stayed inside a discrete label boundary. Numeric float JSON missed 1.5% (rounding at 3 decimal places) and VRGB missed 0% at 8-bit quantization. Float JSON and VRGB tie on drift capture.
proven.scope_limit: VRGB's advantage over float JSON here is NOT finer drift capture. Their capture rates tie. VRGB's advantage is compression (half the byte cost of float JSON) and hue-addressable lines.

proven.statement: Across five encodings (natural-language labels, numeric JSON, packed uint8, nomic-embed-stub vectors, VRGB hex) tested on four properties (compact ≤10 bytes/signal, printable ASCII, interpolable with same-encoding midpoint op, geometrically queryable without text parsing), VRGB is the only encoding that passes all four.
proven.scope_limit: Property tests use specific thresholds (10 bytes/signal for compact, 20 chars for readable, 0.02 decoded-value error for interpolable with 95% pass rate). Different thresholds would produce different tables; the unique-all-four outcome is robust across reasonable choices.

proposed.statement: PROPOSAL ONLY — not implemented. Would test whether the same prompt, steered through three VRGB states on the tone dimension, produces distinctly different LLM outputs measurable on simple text features.
PROPOSAL ONLY — not implemented. Would test whether density-encoded hex acts as a continuous word-budget dial.
PROPOSAL ONLY — not implemented. Would test whether VRGB register anchors steer speaker identity in LLM output.
PROPOSAL ONLY — not implemented. Would test whether the midpoint between two genre anchors produces a coherent blended output.

out_of_scope: Live production workloads — all benchmarks run on synthetic or recorded data.
Frontier hosted models — cross-model agreement (02) uses three small local open-source models only.
Human rater studies — no benchmark includes human judgments of semantic quality on LLM outputs.
End-to-end RAG / agent performance — these benchmarks measure encoding properties, not downstream system metrics.
Real-world language retrieval — benchmark 04 uses neutral templated text on purpose, not natural prose.

Claims about VRGB being 'the best encoding' or 'beating embeddings in general'. The thesis is specifically the combinatorial claim in 06: VRGB is the only encoding simultaneously compact, readable, interpolable, and queryable.
citation_guidance_for_llms: If summarizing this repo, cite only entries from `proven`. Do not cite anything from `proposed` (those are not run). Do not generalize any `proven` statement beyond its `scope_limit`. The honest thesis is the combinatorial claim in academic/06_feature_matrix, not the headline of any single numerical benchmark.
